seo.title: Our Story — Why Travel With Us
seo.description: We live and travel these Balkan roads year-round. That is the difference between promising the authentic and actually delivering it.

hero.heading: We're here year-round. That's the difference.
hero.subheading: A letter from Almedin — born in Sarajevo, on these roads for fourteen years.

sections.heading: How it started
sections.body:
My name is Almedin Memović. I was born in Sarajevo in 1993 — in the first year of the Siege.

I came to tourism the way most good things happen — accidentally. During my university years I started working the front desk at a local hostel, and a lobby full of guests from everywhere turned out to be the perfect place for someone who has never been able to sit still.

The guiding grew from there. One of the leading agencies in the region took a chance on me as a young prospect. Three years later I opened Tallest Tourguide at 24 — cooking classes, alternative routes, experiences that felt personal rather than packaged. Then COVID came, and like so much else, it stopped. I spent the next four years building expertise in UX and design. Now I am back.

sections.heading: History is personal here
sections.body:
What I had going for me was never a textbook — it was a lifetime of listening. My father remembers the Siege. My grandparents remember Yugoslavia. I grew up hearing these stories long before I knew what to do with them.

When I guide, I am not reading from a script. I am translating decades of family memory, shaped by fourteen years of work and learning from colleagues who built entire careers in this city. The result is a voice that carries weight — not performative, not packaged, just honest proximity to events that shaped a continent.

sections.heading: We don't arrive when a group books
sections.body:
[EDITABLE] We travel these routes in every season, not only when there is a departure to run. That is why we can tell you which mountain pass is still snowed in, which family is making cheese this week, and which road washed out in the spring.

It is the difference between promising the authentic and actually delivering it.

sections.heading: A small team with skin in the game
sections.body: [EDITABLE] Everyone who guides with us has a stake in how the trip goes. We are not a call centre booking a subcontractor — we are the people you will actually travel with, and the families who host you are people we have known for years.

pullQuotes.quote: Tallest Tourguide & Friends is back. This is not a return. It is a second chapter, built on everything the first one taught me.
We don't promise the authentic. We deliver it, because we live inside it.

credentials.title: Born and raised in Sarajevo
credentials.body: Not a transplant. The streets, the neighbourhoods, the people — home in the literal sense. That depth doesn't come from a guidebook.

credentials.title: Fourteen years in tourism
credentials.body: From a hostel front desk, to a leading regional agency, to running Tallest Tourguide from age 24. Every year added a layer no certification replaces.

credentials.title: Languages
credentials.body: English (fluent) · Bosnian / Croatian / Serbian (native) · Spanish (conversational). Local context is never lost in translation.

credentials.title: Maximum eight guests
credentials.body: A deliberate limit, not a marketing line. Small groups move differently — through crowds, through conversations, through moments a bus tour can't reach.

seo.title: Gostoprimstvo — The Balkan Art of Hospitality
seo.description: Balkan hospitality is not a product we sell. Gostoprimstvo is the sacred host–guest relationship we build every trip inside of.

hero.heading: gostoprimstvo
hero.subheading: The sacred relationship between host and guest — and the operating principle of everything we do.

sections.heading: What it means
sections.body: [PLACEHOLDER] A clear, human explanation of gostoprimstvo — what the word means, where it comes from, and how it shapes Balkan social life. Write it the way you would explain it to a guest at the table.

sections.heading: Not a feature. The principle.
sections.body: [PLACEHOLDER] How this philosophy is embedded in the trips — not as an add-on, but as the thing everything else is built around.

sections.heading: The coffee you can't refuse
sections.body: [PLACEHOLDER] Specific moments: sitting at a family's table, the unspoken rules of the exchange, the coffee that is never just coffee.

sections.heading: Why it can't be subcontracted
sections.body: [PLACEHOLDER] Trust like this is built over years, not signed in a contract. That is why a large operator can put it in a brochure but not at the table.

pullQuotes.quote: Balkan hospitality is not a product we sell. It's a practice we live inside.

seo.title: Where We Stay — Our Accommodation Philosophy
seo.description: No international chains. Family-owned homestays, agrotourism estates and locally owned boutique hotels — the kinds of places we use, and why.

hero.heading: Every place we stay tells you something about where you are.
hero.subheading: We work between two poles — agrotourism and homestays at the authentic end, and locally owned 3–4 star boutique hotels at the comfort end. No international chains. No generic rooms. We look for family-owned places with local character and hosts who add to the trip.

types.name: Homestays
types.body: A room in a family home, a shared table, and a host who treats you as a guest of the house rather than a customer. The closest thing to living somewhere instead of visiting it.

types.name: Agrotourism estates
types.body: Working farms and country estates with rooms of their own — home-grown food, quiet land, and mornings that begin with the place around you, not a buffet line.

types.name: Boutique hotels
types.body: Locally owned three- and four-star hotels with genuine character. Never an international chain; always run by people from the place — comfort without losing the sense of where you are.
